title: The Ironies of Citizenship, Naturalization and Integration in Industrialized Countries By Thomas Janoski Cambridge University Press, 2010, xv + 336. $29.99 paper
abstract: The issues of nationality and naturalization and the development of related policies are hotly debated topics in politics across many industrialized countries, and substantial cross-national differences in naturalization policies exist. Whereas some countries opt for liberal nationality policies that allow immigrants to quickly become citizens, other countries install restrictive naturalization policies that make it hard for immigrants to become full citizen of their host country. Janoski's book adds to this ongoing debate about cross-national differences in naturalization policies by presenting two main explanations for such differences: a country's colonial history and the success of left and green parties. The colonization explanation is based on a regime theory represented by the following types: (1. colonizing countries (such as Austria, France, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom) that have familiarized people all over the world with their customs and languages will have moderately high rates of naturalization in proportion to their colonization throughout the world; (2. settler countries (Australia, Canada, the United States and New Zealand) whose indigenous population decline causes labor shortages will solve this labor shortage problem by immigration and naturalization; and (3. noncolonizing countries or countries that do not reach the so-called social control stage of colonization (Belgium, Germany, Ireland, Italy, Japan, Switzerland) will have no rationale to accept immigrants.
